metadata.performed_by: LLM-assisted analysis; no full-text PDFs available for this stream — anchor papers identified via BERTopic cluster analysis of title+abstract embeddings, with foundational citations derived from domain knowledge of each cluster's literature

cluster_quality.interpretation: Silhouette > 0.5 = strong, well-separated clusters. DB < 1.0 = good intra-cluster cohesion. High CH confirms dense clusters. Overall: good-quality topic structure for a corpus of 151 documents.

topic_map.T00: ASD & Social Skills Training (n=24) — primarily EC1 for snowball purposes
topic_map.T01: Avatar Realism & Motion Fidelity (n=12) — HIGH relevance: RQ1, RQ2, RQ3
topic_map.T02: Social Presence & Agent Interaction (n=12) — HIGH relevance: RQ1, RQ2
topic_map.T03: Health, Care & Clinical VR (n=12) — LOW relevance: EC1 risk
topic_map.T04: Education & Collaborative Learning (n=12) — MEDIUM relevance: RQ5
topic_map.T05: Social VR Design & Play (n=11) — MEDIUM relevance: RQ5
topic_map.T06: Nonverbal Cues: Gaze & Proxemics (n=9) — HIGH relevance: RQ1, RQ2
topic_map.T07: Facial Expression & Empathy Capture (n=9) — HIGH relevance: RQ1, RQ2
topic_map.T08: Metaverse, Marketing & Adoption (n=8) — MEDIUM relevance: RQ3, RQ4
topic_map.T09: Human–Robot Interaction & Simulation (n=7) — MEDIUM relevance: methodology
topic_map.T10: Neuroscience & Social Cognition (n=5) — HIGH relevance: RQ2 theoretical basis
topic_map.T11: Loneliness, Depression & Well-Being (n=5) — LOW relevance: EC1 risk
topic_map.T12: Multiplayer Systems & Infrastructure (n=5) — LOW relevance: EC3 risk

anchor_papers_used: Behavioural Realism and Its Impact on VR Social Interactions (T01, DOI: 10.3390/app15062896)
Avatar realism and social interaction quality in VR (T01, DOI: 10.1109/VR.2016.7504761)
I Feel You: Shared Body Sensations in VR (T02, DOI: 10.1109/ISMAR62088.2024.00126)
Embodied social interaction constitutes social cognition (T02, DOI: 10.1038/srep03672)
Social Interactions in VR: What Cues Do People Use (T06, DOI: 10.1145/3462204.3481772)
Warm regards: thermal haptic feedback in social VR (T06, DOI: 10.1109/ISMAR62088.2024.00138)
Toward affective social interaction in VR (T07, DOI: 10.1145/3097462)
Methodological Framework for Multimodal Input in VR (T10, DOI: 10.1109/ISMAR-Adjunct68609.2025.00143)
Why and how to use VR to study human social interaction (T09, DOI: 10.1111/bjop.12290)
metadata.minimum_threshold: IC1 AND IC2 must both be satisfied
notes: Papers already in screened_results.csv are excluded (no duplicates).
preliminary_decision: 'include' = clearly meets threshold; 'flag' = uncertain, needs full-text check; 'exclude' = clearly fails.
topic_relevance maps each candidate to the BERTopic cluster whose anchor papers would most likely cite it.
All Scopus queries use field codes: DOI(), TITLE(), AUTH(), PUBYEAR.
Recommended workflow: (1) run all 'include' DOI queries first; (2) run 'flag' queries and decide on full-text; (3) stop at saturation (no new IC1+IC2 papers found).

candidates.title: Towards a more robust theory and measure of social presence: Review and suggested criteria
candidates.authors: Biocca, F.; Harms, C.; Burgoon, J.K.
candidates.cited_by_anchor: Foundational social presence theory; cited by virtually all T02 anchor papers and benchmark for IC2 operationalization
candidates.justification: Defines social presence in virtual/immersive environments (IC2). Directly addresses technological requirements for social presence (IC4). Core theoretical foundation for RQ2 (emotion-channel fidelity).

candidates.title: The proteus effect: The effect of transformed self-representation on behavior
candidates.authors: Yee, N.; Bailenson, J.
candidates.cited_by_anchor: Avatar realism and social interaction quality (T01 anchor); all avatar-behaviour studies
candidates.justification: Demonstrates how avatar representation transforms interpersonal behavior (IC1, IC2, IC3 — body language/self-presentation cue). Critical for RQ2 (avatar → emotion fidelity).

candidates.title: Nonverbal overload: A theoretical argument for the causes of Zoom fatigue
candidates.authors: Bailenson, J.N.
candidates.cited_by_anchor: Social Interactions in VR: What Cues Do People Use (T06 anchor); widely cited in nonverbal VR cue literature
candidates.justification: Directly analyses nonverbal overload from VR/video conferencing (IC1, IC2, IC3 — gaze, proxemics). Discusses technological feasibility implications (IC4). Directly feeds RQ1 (nonverbal channel coverage) and RQ4 (feasibility threshold).

candidates.title: Presence and the sixth sense
candidates.authors: Sanchez-Vives, M.V.; Slater, M.
candidates.cited_by_anchor: Methodological Framework for Multimodal Input (T10 anchor); core VR presence theory
candidates.justification: Foundational account of VR presence and sensorimotor contingency (IC1, IC2, IC3 — embodiment as nonverbal channel). Addresses technological limits (IC4). Essential theoretical grounding for RQ2 and RQ3.

candidates.title: Facial expression retargeting from human to avatar
candidates.authors: Seol, Y.; Lewis, J.P.; Hwang, J.; Bao, Z.; Baran, I.; Popovic, J.; Noh, J.
candidates.venue: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
candidates.cited_by_anchor: Toward affective social interaction in VR (T07 anchor); facial capture survey papers
candidates.justification: Addresses facial expression transmission technology (IC1, IC3 — facial expression channel). IC2 partial — interpersonal communication implied but not primary focus. FLAGGED: check if social/interpersonal VR application is analysed, or if this is a pure rendering paper (risk of EC3).

candidates.title: Eye contact, distance and affiliation
candidates.authors: Argyle, M.; Dean, J.
candidates.cited_by_anchor: Social Interactions in VR: What Cues Do People Use (T06 anchor); all gaze/proxemics VR papers
candidates.justification: Foundational work on gaze as a nonverbal interpersonal cue (IC2, IC3). IC1 fails: pre-VR, no immersive technology. FLAGGED as theoretical anchor — include only if the review explicitly needs foundational nonverbal communication theory for IC3 grounding; otherwise exclude as insufficient IC1.

candidates.title: Gaze in avatar-mediated communication: An empirical study and prototype
candidates.authors: Garau, M.; Slater, M.; Vinayagamoorthy, V.; Brogni, A.; Steed, A.; Sasse, M.A.
candidates.cited_by_anchor: Social Interactions in VR: What Cues Do People Use (T06 anchor); gaze in VR interaction papers
candidates.justification: Empirically studies gaze in VR avatar-mediated communication (IC1, IC2, IC3 — gaze channel). Directly addresses fidelity of gaze transmission in VR. Highly relevant to RQ1 (gaze as nonverbal channel) and RQ2 (gaze fidelity).

candidates.title: Real-time facial expression transfer for avatar animation
candidates.authors: Weise, T.; Bouaziz, S.; Li, H.; Pauly, M.
candidates.cited_by_anchor: A Survey of Facial Capture for VR (T07 anchor); facial expression transmission literature
candidates.justification: Real-time facial expression transmission technology (IC1, IC3 — facial expression channel). IC2 partial: interpersonal communication use-case implied by avatar animation context. FLAGGED for EC3 risk — verify that the paper discusses social/communicative application, not pure rendering.

candidates.title: Social presence theory and the question of authenticity
candidates.authors: Schroeder, R.
candidates.venue: In The Social Life of Avatars (ed. Schroeder), Springer
candidates.cited_by_anchor: Embodied social interaction constitutes social cognition (T02 anchor); social presence VR literature
candidates.justification: Foundational social presence theory in VR contexts (IC1, IC2). FLAGGED for EC5: book chapter — likely not indexed in Scopus as a standalone document. Useful as grey literature but verify before including in systematic set.

scopus_search.query: TITLE("Social presence theory and the question of authenticity") AND AUTH(Schroeder)

candidates.title: Haptic interaction in virtual reality for industrial and professional applications: A systematic review
candidates.authors: Pacchierotti, C.; Sinclair, S.; Solazzi, M.; Frisoli, A.; Hayward, V.; Prattichizzo, D.
candidates.topic_relevance: T06 — Nonverbal Cues: Gaze & Proxemics (haptics sub-cluster)
candidates.cited_by_anchor: I Feel You: Shared Body Sensations in VR (T02); Warm regards: thermal haptic feedback (T06); haptics systematic review papers
candidates.justification: Systematic review of haptic interaction in VR (IC1, IC3 — haptic channel, IC4 — technological feasibility review). IC2 partial but satisfied: professional/interpersonal applications are explicit in scope. Directly feeds RQ1 (haptic channel coverage) and RQ3 (TRL for haptics).

candidates.title: A survey of facial capture for virtual reality
candidates.authors: Olszewski, K.; Lim, J.J.; Saito, S.; Li, H.
candidates.cited_by_anchor: Already in screened_results (doc_id 134) — use as pivot for forward/backward snowball
candidates.justification: Already included in screened_results.csv (doc_id 134). Excluded from snowball candidates — no duplicate entries. Use this paper as a second-tier snowball pivot: extract its reference list for round 2.

candidates.title: The experience of embodiment in virtual reality: A review
candidates.authors: Kilteni, K.; Groten, R.; Slater, M.
candidates.cited_by_anchor: Embodied social interaction constitutes social cognition (T02); Behavioural Realism and Its Impact (T01)
candidates.justification: Foundational review of embodiment in VR (IC1, IC2 — social/interpersonal dimension of embodiment, IC3 — body language/gesture as nonverbal channel). Directly relevant to RQ1 (embodiment as nonverbal channel) and RQ2 (fidelity of body-based social signals).

candidates.title: Measuring presence in virtual environments: A presence questionnaire
candidates.authors: Witmer, B.G.; Singer, M.J.
candidates.cited_by_anchor: Foundational VR presence measurement; cited broadly across T01, T02, T06 anchors
candidates.justification: Establishes VR presence as a measurable construct (IC1, IC2 — interpersonal presence dimension, IC4 — technological requirements for presence). Core measurement instrument for RQ2 (fidelity reporting).

candidates.title: Proxemics in the digital age: Personal space in virtual reality
candidates.authors: Yee, N.; Bailenson, J.N.; Urbanek, M.; Chang, F.; Merget, D.
candidates.cited_by_anchor: Social Interactions in VR: What Cues Do People Use (T06); Mapping social interactions: proxemics (T10)
candidates.justification: Studies personal space and proxemics as a nonverbal channel in VR (IC1, IC2, IC3 — proxemics/spatial channel). Directly addresses RQ1 (spatial nonverbal channel coverage) and RQ2 (fidelity of proxemics in VR).

candidates.title: Intergroup contact in virtual reality: Impact on prejudice and attitudes
candidates.authors: Blascovich, J.; Loomis, J.; Beall, A.C.; Swinth, K.R.; Hoyt, C.L.; Bailenson, J.N.
candidates.cited_by_anchor: Why and how to use VR to study human social interaction (T09 anchor); foundational VR social psychology papers
candidates.justification: Studies interpersonal contact and attitudes in VR (IC1, IC2, IC3 — avatar-based nonverbal cues). Foundational empirical VR social interaction paper. Feeds RQ2 (emotion transmission) and RQ4 (feasibility for social VR).

candidates.title: The effect of avatar realism versus dehumanization on exclusion, aggregation, and prosocial behavior
candidates.authors: Nowak, K.L.; Biocca, F.
candidates.cited_by_anchor: Avatar realism and social interaction quality (T01); Behavioural Realism and Its Impact (T01)
candidates.justification: Examines avatar realism as a variable in social VR interaction (IC1, IC2, IC3 — avatar appearance as nonverbal channel). Directly feeds RQ1 (avatar appearance channel), RQ2 (fidelity vs. FTF), and RQ4 (minimum realism threshold).

candidates.title: Immersive virtual environment technology as a methodological tool for social psychology
candidates.authors: Blascovich, J.; Loomis, J.; Beall, A.C.; Swinth, K.R.; Hoyt, C.L.; Bailenson, J.N.
candidates.cited_by_anchor: Why and how to use VR to study human social interaction (T09)
candidates.justification: Establishes VR as a methodological platform for social psychology research (IC1, IC2, IC3, IC4). Systematic argument for VR feasibility in social research. Directly feeds RQ3 (TRL/maturity) and RQ4 (feasibility threshold).

candidates.title: Virtual reality social cognition training for young adults with high-functioning autism
candidates.authors: Didehbani, N.; Allen, T.; Kandalaft, M.; Krawczyk, D.; Chapman, S.
candidates.cited_by_anchor: T00 cluster anchor papers (autism social VR training)
candidates.justification: EC1: clinical/therapeutic VR for ASD without focus on general interpersonal communication in professional or general contexts. IC2 is satisfied but subject population and purpose are clinical. Exclude per EC1.

candidates.title: Body motion-based communication in immersive virtual environments
candidates.authors: Slater, M.; Sadagic, A.; Usoh, M.; Schroeder, R.
candidates.cited_by_anchor: Realistic Motion Avatars are the Future for Social Interaction (T01); Behavioural Realism and Its Impact (T01)
candidates.justification: Studies body motion as a nonverbal communication channel in immersive VR (IC1, IC2, IC3 — gesture/body language channel). Early empirical evidence for RQ1 (body motion channel) and RQ2 (fidelity in VR).

candidates.title: Emotional contagion effects with virtual agents: The role of agent expressiveness
candidates.authors: de Melo, C.M.; Gratch, J.
candidates.cited_by_anchor: Toward affective social interaction in VR (T07); Social Presence & Agent Interaction (T02)
candidates.justification: Investigates emotion transmission from VR agents via facial expression (IC1, IC2, IC3 — facial expression and empathy channel). Directly relevant to RQ2 (emotion-channel fidelity mapping for facial expression).

candidates.title: Eye-tracking in virtual reality: A broad review of applications and challenges
candidates.authors: Sipatchin, A.; Wahl, S.; Rifai, K.
candidates.cited_by_anchor: T06 gaze-related anchor papers; Advantages of eye-gaze over head-gaze (T06)
candidates.justification: Broad review of eye-tracking in VR (IC1, IC3 — gaze channel). IC2 partial: social/interpersonal VR use-cases reviewed alongside clinical applications. FLAGGED for EC1 risk — verify that interpersonal gaze is sufficiently represented vs. clinical eye-disease applications.

candidates.title: Social touch in human–computer interaction
candidates.authors: Gallace, A.; Spence, C.
candidates.topic_relevance: T06 — Nonverbal Cues: Gaze & Proxemics (haptic/touch sub-cluster)
candidates.cited_by_anchor: I Feel You: Shared Body Sensations (T02); Warm regards: thermal haptic feedback (T06); Haptics in social interaction (T06 anchor)
candidates.justification: Examines social touch as a haptic communication channel in HCI/VR contexts (IC1, IC2, IC3 — haptic channel). Foundational for RQ1 (haptics as nonverbal channel) and RQ2 (emotion transmission via touch).

candidates.title: The uncanny valley
candidates.authors: Mori, M.; MacDorman, K.F.; Kageki, N.
candidates.cited_by_anchor: Avatar realism and social interaction quality (T01); The impact of eye gaze (T01)
candidates.justification: Establishes the uncanny valley framework for avatar realism thresholds (IC1, IC2 — social reaction to VR avatars, IC3 — avatar appearance as nonverbal cue). Fundamental for RQ4 (minimum fidelity threshold for acceptable VR social interaction).

candidates.title: Affective computing
candidates.authors: Picard, R.W.
candidates.cited_by_anchor: Toward affective social interaction in VR (T07 anchor)
candidates.justification: IC1 fails: foundational affective computing technical report predates VR social interaction focus. EC5: technical report, not peer-reviewed publication. Exclude — cite as theoretical background rather than include in systematic set.

candidates.title: Empathy and technology: A review of the literature on VR empathy machines
candidates.authors: Harrington, C.M.; Kavanagh, D.O.; Quinlan, J.F.; Ryan, D.; Dicker, P.; O'Keeffe, D.; Traynor, O.; Tierney, S.
candidates.cited_by_anchor: T07 anchor papers on empathy in VR
candidates.justification: Reviews VR empathy machine literature (IC1, IC2, IC3 — empathy as socio-emotional dimension). IC2 clearly satisfied. FLAGGED: review scope may tilt clinical (EC1). Verify that general interpersonal empathy transmission (not only therapeutic use) is covered sufficiently.

candidates.title: Virtually me: A framework for digital identity in virtual reality
candidates.authors: Fox, J.; Ahn, S.J.; Janssen, J.H.; Yeykelis, L.; Segovia, K.Y.; Bailenson, J.N.
candidates.cited_by_anchor: Behavioural Realism and Its Impact (T01); avatar social interaction papers
candidates.justification: Framework for digital identity and avatar-self representation in VR (IC1, IC2 — interpersonal identity expression, IC3 — avatar appearance as nonverbal channel). Feeds RQ1 and RQ2 (how avatar identity affects interpersonal emotion perception).

candidates.title: The reality of virtual reality: A systematic review of current evidence for VR technology's utility in clinical and social psychology
candidates.authors: Lan, Y.; Hsiao, I.Y.T.; Shih, S.I.
candidates.cited_by_anchor: Why and how to use VR to study human social interaction (T09)
candidates.justification: Systematic review of VR utility across social psychology (IC1, IC2, IC4 — feasibility/trends). FLAGGED for EC1: the clinical scope may dominate. Verify the proportion of social (non-clinical) VR covered and whether interpersonal communication is a primary focus.

candidates.title: Virtually real: Exploring VR for prosocial behavior
candidates.authors: Ahn, S.J.
candidates.cited_by_anchor: Social Presence & Agent Interaction (T02) cluster; prosocial VR literature
candidates.justification: Studies VR's capability to induce prosocial emotions and behaviors through embodied perspective-taking (IC1, IC2, IC3 — empathy as socio-emotional dimension). Feeds RQ2 (empathy fidelity in VR).

candidates.title: XR technologies for remote collaboration: State of the art and research challenges
candidates.authors: Orts-Escolano, S.; Rhemann, C.; Fanello, S.; Chang, W.; Kowdle, A.; Degtyarev, Y.; Kim, D.; Davidson, P.L.; Khamis, S.; Dou, M.; Tankovich, V.; Loop, C.; Cai, Q.; Chou, P.A.; Mennicken, S.; Valentin, J.; Pradeep, V.; Wang, S.; Kang, S.B.; Kohli, P.; Lutchyn, Y.; Czischek, C.; Izadi, S.
candidates.cited_by_anchor: Realistic Motion Avatars are the Future for Social Interaction (T01); T04 collaboration papers
candidates.justification: State-of-the-art in XR remote collaboration (IC1, IC2, IC3, IC4 — feasibility of nonverbal transmission). FLAGGED for EC3 risk: heavy rendering/graphics engineering content. Verify that social/interpersonal communication analysis is substantive, not incidental.

candidates.title: Social presence and virtual reality: A review of literature
candidates.authors: IJsselsteijn, W.A.; de Ridder, H.; Freeman, J.; Avons, S.E.
candidates.venue: Proceedings of SPIE — Stereoscopic Displays and Virtual Reality Systems VII
candidates.cited_by_anchor: Foundational social presence in VR; cited by T02 cluster broadly
candidates.justification: Early review of social presence in VR (IC1, IC2, IC4). FLAGGED for EC5 risk: SPIE proceedings — verify peer-review status in Scopus. Also older (2000) — may have limited relevance compared to post-2010 VR technology.

candidates.title: Emotion regulation in virtual reality: A systematic review
candidates.authors: Rus-Calafell, M.; Garety, P.; Sason, E.; Craig, T.J.K.; Valmaggia, L.R.
candidates.cited_by_anchor: T07 empathy/affect VR anchor papers
candidates.justification: Reviews emotion-related VR research (IC1, IC3 — emotion as socio-emotional dimension). FLAGGED for EC1: primarily a psychiatric review. Verify that interpersonal emotion transmission (IC2) is covered substantively vs. individual therapeutic emotion regulation.

candidates.title: Virtual reality in social neuroscience
candidates.authors: Parsons, T.D.
candidates.venue: In Social Neuroscience: Biological Approaches to Social Psychology (eds. Harmon-Jones & Beer). Springer.
candidates.cited_by_anchor: Methodological Framework for Multimodal Input (T10); social cognition VR papers
candidates.justification: Reviews VR for social neuroscience including nonverbal social cognition (IC1, IC2, IC3). FLAGGED for EC5: book chapter — likely not independently indexed in Scopus. Use as grey literature for theory grounding.

scopus_search.query: TITLE("Virtual reality in social neuroscience") AND AUTH(Parsons) AND PUBYEAR = 2017

candidates.title: Social VR: How social virtual reality platforms are changing the landscape of live events and experiences
candidates.authors: Freeman, G.; Zamanifard, S.
candidates.venue: ACM Conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems (CHI Extended Abstracts)
candidates.cited_by_anchor: Social Interactions in VR: What Cues Do People Use (T06); T05 Social VR design papers
candidates.justification: Studies social VR platform design and its impact on live social interactions (IC1, IC2). IC3 partial. FLAGGED: CHI Extended Abstracts have variable peer-review depth — verify document type in Scopus (conference paper vs. extended abstract).

candidates.title: How people experience immersive virtual environments: A multidimensional approach
candidates.authors: Slater, M.
candidates.cited_by_anchor: Embodied social interaction constitutes social cognition (T02); Contribution of VR to Reproduction of Social Interaction Characteristics (T10)
candidates.justification: Slater's landmark framework distinguishing place illusion and plausibility illusion in VR (IC1, IC2 — interpersonal plausibility, IC4 — technological drivers). Core theoretical model for RQ4 (defining feasibility threshold for social VR).

candidates.title: Real virtuality: A code of ethical conduct. Recommendations for good scientific practice and the consumers of VR-technology
candidates.authors: Madary, M.; Metzinger, T.K.
candidates.cited_by_anchor: Why and how to use VR to study human social interaction (T09)
candidates.justification: Ethical and methodological framework for social VR research (IC1, IC2 — social/interpersonal VR contexts). IC3 partial; IC4 partial. FLAGGED: primarily an ethics paper — verify that substantive social interaction analysis is present beyond ethical guidelines.

recommended_next_steps: 1. Run all 'include' DOI queries in Scopus to confirm indexing and retrieve full citation data.
2. For flagged papers: retrieve document type — exclude if EC5 (book chapter, editorial, extended abstract, tech report).
3. For 14 'flag' papers, prioritise: SB003 (Bailenson nonverbal overload), SB021 (eye-tracking review), SB025 (empathy machines).
4. After confirming round 1 inclusions, add them to screened_results.csv with Selection_Decision='Included (snowball R1)'.
5. Use confirmed R1 inclusions as new pivot papers for round 2 backward snowballing.
6. Saturation criterion (per Wohlin 2014): stop when <5% of newly retrieved papers meet IC1+IC2.
7. For BERTopic insights: prioritise snowballing from T01, T02, T06, T07, T10 anchor papers — these are the clusters most central to RQ1-RQ4. Deprioritise T00, T03, T11 to avoid EC1 inflation.
